section.title: Greeting
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens unlike Paul's others: he names himself not an apostle but a prisoner, and he addresses not only Philemon but his household and the whole church that meets in his house. Every word quietly prepares the delicate appeal that will follow.
section.thesis: Paul sets aside his apostolic rank and comes as a prisoner and fellow worker, gathering Philemon, his family, and the church into one greeting of grace and peace, so that a private matter about a runaway slave is framed from the start as a family matter of the gospel.
section.frameNote: One movement in three parts: the senders, Paul and Timothy; the recipients, Philemon, Apphia, Archippus, and the church; and the blessing of grace and peace.

units.label: Senders, recipients, and blessing
units.note: a prisoner writes to a household and a church

verses.text: Paul, a {{desmios|prisoner}} of Christ Jesus, and Timothy our brother, To Philemon our beloved {{synergos|fellow worker}},
to Apphia our sister, to Archippus our fellow soldier, and to the church that meets at your house:
{{charis|Grace}} and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

desmios.gloss: A prisoner, one held in chains or bonds. In most of his letters Paul opens by claiming his title as an apostle; here he opens as a prisoner of Christ Jesus. He appeals from weakness and love rather than rank, and his chains, mentioned again and again, set the humble tone of the whole letter.

synergos.gloss: A fellow worker or co-laborer. Paul greets Philemon not as a subordinate to be commanded but as a partner in the work of the gospel. The word lays the relational groundwork for the request Paul is about to make, appealing to a shared labor and love.

charis.gloss: Grace, undeserved favor and kindness. It is Paul's customary greeting, but it is freighted here: the entire letter is a plea that the grace Philemon has received from God would now flow out of him toward a returning slave who has no claim on him.

basic.context: Paul is in prison, and from his cell he writes a short, personal letter to Philemon, a well-off Christian in the town of Colossae whose home hosts a church. Paul had led Philemon to faith. The whole letter concerns Onesimus, Philemon's slave, who ran away, met Paul in prison, and became a Christian. These first three verses are simply the greeting: Paul names the senders, the people he is writing to, and prays grace and peace over them all.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real letter to a real man about a real and awkward situation. Paul could have pulled rank as an apostle and given orders. Instead he calls himself a prisoner and a fellow worker, and he lets the whole church listen in. The way he opens teaches us how the gospel reshapes even how we ask people for hard things.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls himself a desmios, a prisoner in chains, not an apostle. He calls Philemon a synergos, a fellow worker, a partner rather than an underling. And he opens with charis, grace, the same grace he will soon ask Philemon to show. The words are gentle on purpose.
basic.typology: There is no formal type here, but the shape of things points ahead. A greeting of grace and peace flows from the Father and the Lord Jesus, the very grace that will be asked to travel from a master to a slave, picturing how God's grace to us is meant to move through us to others.
basic.christ: Everything is anchored in Christ Jesus. Paul is His prisoner, the grace and peace come through Him, and the reconciliation the letter seeks is only possible because of Him. Christ is named in every verse of this greeting, the ground on which enemies can become family.
basic.god: God is Father, the source of grace and peace, and the One who binds strangers into one household. He is generous, giving grace freely, and He is the One from whom all true peace between people flows.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 4:9 calls Onesimus one of the Colossians, tying this letter to that church. Ephesians 3:1 and 4:1 show Paul again calling himself a prisoner of Christ. Numbers 6 lies behind the ancient blessing of grace and peace.
basic.oneStory: The gospel gathers people into one family under one Father. This small greeting, sent from a prison to a house church, is a snapshot of the whole Bible's aim: grace from God creating a people at peace with Him and with one another.

context.literary: The standard opening of an ancient letter, sender, recipient, and greeting, but shaped by the gospel and by Paul's purpose. Notably Paul drops his usual title of apostle and calls himself a prisoner, softening the ground for a request rather than a command. He also widens the audience beyond Philemon to his household and church, so the private appeal carries public weight.
context.historical: Paul writes from imprisonment, most likely in Rome around AD 60 to 62, as a companion piece to Colossians. Philemon is a Christian of some means in Colossae; his home is large enough to host the church. Apphia is likely his wife and Archippus, called a fellow soldier, likely his son or a church leader. In the Roman world a runaway slave faced severe punishment, which makes the coming appeal costly and daring.
context.canonical: The shortest of Paul's letters and the most personal, yet it earns its place in Scripture by showing the gospel at work in the hardest of relationships. It stands as a living case study of the reconciliation the rest of the New Testament proclaims.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To open a delicate letter with warmth and humility, to frame himself as a fellow worker rather than a commander, and to draw the whole church in as witnesses, all so that the appeal for Onesimus will be received as an act of gospel love.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greeting describes Paul's manner and relationships; it does not command anything yet. The abiding pattern it models is a gentle, grace-soaked way of approaching others, especially when we must ask something hard of them.

debates.question: Why does Paul call himself a prisoner rather than an apostle here?

views.label: A deliberate appeal to love, not authority
views.case: Paul chooses not to pull rank because he wants Philemon's obedience to be free and glad, not compelled. Naming himself a prisoner evokes sympathy and sets the humble tone the whole letter needs.

views.label: A simple reflection of his circumstances
views.case: Paul is in fact in chains, and the title honestly names where he writes from and why his ministry now takes this personal, pleading form.

debates.bearing: Either way, the self-description shapes the letter: Paul comes low, as a prisoner and partner, so that reconciliation will be won by love rather than forced by rank.

study.originalLanguages: Three words set the tone. Paul is a desmios, a prisoner, not an apostle, so he appeals rather than orders. Philemon is a synergos, a fellow worker, a partner in the gospel rather than a subordinate. And the greeting is charis, grace, the very grace Paul will ask Philemon to extend to a returning slave. The vocabulary is warm and level, preparing the way for a hard request made in love.

typology.type: Grace and peace flowing from the Father through the Son
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of the whole gospel: grace received from God is meant to travel through us to others, here from a master toward a slave, as it will be asked in the rest of the letter.

study.christ: Christ Jesus is named in every verse of this greeting. Paul is His prisoner, grace and peace come from Him alongside the Father, and the reconciliation the letter seeks rests entirely on Him. Only because Christ has made peace between God and sinners can Paul dare to ask that a runaway slave and his master be made brothers. The whole appeal stands or falls on the peace Jesus has already secured.
study.god: God is revealed as Father, the fountain of grace and peace, and as the One who gathers unlikely people, a prisoner, a wealthy master, his family, and a whole church, into a single household. He gives grace freely, and He is the source of the peace between people that the letter will pursue.

crossReferences.note: Paul calls Onesimus a faithful and beloved brother, one of you, tying this letter directly to the church at Colossae.
Paul again names himself a prisoner of Christ Jesus, a title he wears with purpose.
The ancient priestly blessing of grace and peace that stands behind Paul's opening prayer.
God gives the ministry of reconciliation, the very work this small letter puts on display.

study.oneStory: The gospel makes one family out of strangers under one Father. This greeting, carried from a prison cell to a house church, is a small window onto the Bible's whole aim: grace from God creating a reconciled people, at peace with Him and with one another. The appeal that follows is simply this grace working itself out in one difficult relationship.

sermon.title: Terms of Endearment
sermon.why: The opening message of Begg's series A Study in Philemon covers Philemon 1:1-7, working through the greeting and Paul's warm terms of endearment, and showing how the gospel shapes the way Paul approaches Philemon. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement's opening.

quiz.q: Why does Paul call himself a prisoner rather than an apostle at the start of this letter?

options.text: He appeals from love and humility rather than pulling rank, to win a free and glad response.
options.note: Paul deliberately comes low, as a prisoner and fellow worker, so reconciliation is won by love, not command.

options.text: He was simply stating that he was in prison at the time.
options.note: True, he was in chains, but the choice of title is deliberate and shapes the whole appeal.

options.text: He had lost his authority as an apostle.
options.note: Paul still holds apostolic authority; he chooses not to lean on it here.

options.text: He wanted Philemon to feel sorry for him and obey out of guilt.
options.note: Paul seeks a willing response of love, not a guilt-driven one.

quiz.q: Who does Paul address in the greeting?

options.text: Philemon, along with Apphia, Archippus, and the whole church that meets in his house.
options.note: Paul widens a private matter to include the household and church as loving witnesses.

options.text: Only Philemon, since the matter was private.
options.note: Philemon is the main recipient, but Paul deliberately draws in his family and church too.
This is a letter to a Christian household and church, not to civic authorities.
The letter is about Onesimus but addressed to Philemon and his church.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray over Philemon and the church?

options.text: Grace and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
options.note: The very grace Paul prays down is the grace he will soon ask Philemon to show Onesimus.

options.text: God's blessing and keeping over them all.
options.note: A true summary of his heart, but Paul's specific words are grace and peace.

options.text: Health and prosperity in this life.
options.note: Paul's blessing is spiritual grace and peace, not material wellbeing.

options.text: Nothing; he moves straight to his demands.
options.note: Paul opens with a warm blessing before any request.

quiz.q: How is Christ present in these three opening verses?

options.text: He is named in every verse, the ground of Paul's imprisonment, the source of grace and peace, and the basis of reconciliation.
options.note: The whole appeal rests on the peace Christ has already secured.

options.text: He is mentioned once, in passing.
options.note: In fact Christ Jesus is named throughout the greeting, not just once.

options.text: He is not mentioned until later in the letter.
options.note: Christ is central from the very first verse.

options.text: He appears only as an example to imitate.
options.note: Christ is far more than an example here; He is the ground of the whole appeal.

questions: Paul could have given orders but chose to appeal as a fellow worker. When you need something hard from someone, do you tend to pull rank or to appeal in love, and what would change if you followed Paul's way?
Paul opens with grace and peace before raising anything difficult. How might beginning your hardest conversations with genuine blessing reshape them?
The greeting draws a whole church into one man's private matter. Who are the people God has placed around you to help carry your relationships toward reconciliation?
Paul calls himself a prisoner of Christ, not merely a prisoner of Rome. How does seeing your own limits and hardships as being in Christ's hands change the way you carry them?
The grace Paul prays down is the grace he will soon ask Philemon to give away. Where is God asking you to pass on to others the grace you have received from Him?

translationNotes.note: The words you and your are singular throughout this letter, addressed to Philemon personally, except in verses 3, 22, and 25, where they widen to the whole gathered church.
The church that meets at your house reflects the early practice of congregations gathering in believers' homes; Philemon's home was large enough to host such a gathering.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philemon 1:1-3. No em dashes appear in this movement's text.
